title: Sussex pond pudding
subtitle: British steamed suet pudding with a whole lemon, butter, and brown sugar inside
excerpt: A Sussex speciality in which a whole unwaxed lemon, packed around with cold butter and soft brown sugar, is encased in suet pastry and steamed for two and a half hours. When the pudding is turned out and sliced, the centre releases a rich, sharp, buttery sauce that pools on the plate. The whole lemon cooks to a tender softness and gives the sauce a bitter marmalade note. Extraordinary flavour from almost no technique.
sourceNotes: Sussex pond pudding is a traditional British steamed suet pudding. Boiled suet puddings with various fillings appear in Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747, Project Gutenberg). Steamed puddings encasing whole citrus fruit with butter and sugar are recorded in British county and household cookery collections of the nineteenth century. The name refers to the pool of sauce that forms when the pudding is turned out and sliced.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pudding can be assembled several hours ahead and refrigerated before steaming. It does not keep well once cooked; serve immediately after turning out.

baking.steamMethod: steamed in a covered saucepan of simmering water for 2.5 hours; keep water topped up and maintain a steady simmer throughout

recipeTools.notes: large enough to hold a 1.2-litre pudding basin with the lid on; place an upturned saucer or folded foil in the base to keep the basin off the bottom
for the double-layer lid; pleat to allow expansion

glossaryTerms.definition: The pudding steams for 2.5 to 3 hours at a steady gentle simmer. Too fierce a boil bounces the basin and can crack the pastry. Check the water level every 30 minutes and top up with boiling water so the pan never boils dry. The pudding is ready when the suet pastry feels firm and set through the paper lid when pressed. The full time is not optional: an understeamed suet pastry will be doughy and grey in the centre. When turned out, the sauce should flow immediately from the centre as the lemon is cut.

content.text: The pudding basin (a 1.2-litre straight-sided heatproof bowl) is the key equipment here.
is not negotiable: suet pastry needs sustained heat to cook through. Keep a kettle of boiling water nearby to top up the pan.

attrs.prepNote: unwaxed; pierce all over with a skewer

content.text: Make the suet pastry: mix the flour, suet, baking powder, and salt together. Add just enough cold water (about 100ml) to bring it to a soft, pliable dough. Butter the pudding basin generously. Roll two-thirds of the pastry into a circle large enough to line the basin with a slight overhang. Lower it in carefully and press it to the sides with no air gaps.

Put half the butter and half the sugar into the lined basin. Pierce the whole lemon all over with a skewer and place it on top. Cover with the remaining butter and sugar. Roll the remaining pastry into a lid, press it over the top, and crimp the edges together firmly to seal. The seal is important: any gap will let the sauce escape during steaming.

Cover the basin with a double layer of baking paper with a pleat in the centre to allow expansion, then a layer of foil. Tie it on securely with string. Lower the basin into a saucepan with an upturned saucer in the base. Pour in boiling water to come halfway up the basin. Cover the pan, bring to a steady simmer, and steam for 2.5 to 3 hours, topping up with boiling water as needed. To serve, remove the paper and foil, run a knife around the edge, and invert onto a deep plate with a rim to catch the sauce.
